metadata.title: Claude, On the Job.
metadata.thesis: You're not behind the machine. You're what the machine is missing.

beats.narration_text: The four-tier picture reframed things for me — I shouldn't compete at Tier 1 retrieval, I should conduct, which is Tier 3 evaluation and Tier 4 problem-formulation and distrust-calibration. I want to start the daily loop on my actual work. Help me: (1) pick one real task I do daily and script how I'd hand it to Claude while reading every line critically; (2) build a checklist for catching confabulation and naming the one thing it got weak; (3) define which facts and judgment calls I must never delegate because I sign my name and bear the consequence.

props.command: The four-tier picture reframed things for me — I shouldn't compete at Tier 1 retrieval, I should conduct, which is Tier 3 evaluation and Tier 4 problem-formulation and distrust-calibration. I want to start the daily loop on my actual work. Help me: (1) pick one real task I do daily and script how I'd hand it to Claude while reading every line critically; (2) build a checklist for catching confabulation and naming the one thing it got weak; (3) define which facts and judgment calls I must never delegate because I sign my name and bear the consequence.
output: no — the durable skills are yours
Tier 1 is the machine's: recall, syntax, retrieval
Tier 4 is yours: judgment, audit, distrust-calibration

beats.narration_text: The tier picture. Four levels. Tier 1: recall, syntax, retrieval — the machine is superhuman here, today. Tier 2: synthesis and pattern-matching — contested space, human and machine both. Tier 3: evaluation, quality judgment — human advantage. You read the output and decide if it is right, if it is good, if it serves the actual goal. Tier 4: problem formulation, audit, distrust-calibration — irreducible. No model decides what problem is worth solving, or whether to trust its own answer. That is yours.

props.heading: Conduct, don't compete.
props.body:
Tier 1: recall · syntax · retrieval — machine (superhuman)
Tier 2: synthesis · pattern-matching — contested
Tier 3: evaluation · quality judgment — human advantage
Tier 4: problem formulation · audit · distrust-calibration — irreducible

No model decides what problem is worth solving. That's yours.

beats.narration_text: What is actually scarce. Employers are not paying extra for the person who can retrieve faster than the model — no one can. What is scarce is conducting: knowing what to ask, reading the output critically, catching the confabulation, deciding what gets handed to a human. That is Tier 3 and 4 work. Competing at Tier 1 with the machine is not the job and it is not the skill that is valued. Conducting the machine is the job.

props.heading: What's actually scarce.
props.body:
Not: retrieving faster than the model.
Scarce: conducting.

— knowing what to ask
— reading output critically
— catching the confabulation
— deciding what reaches a human

Tier 3 and 4. That's what employers pay for.

beats.narration_text: The daily loop. One real work task per day — not a demo, not an experiment. A real thing you actually have to do. Use Claude on it. Read every line of the output. Name one thing it got wrong or weak. That is the practice. Not volume. Not novelty. Judgment-building. You are training your distrust-calibration — knowing when to trust the output and when to override it. That is the skill the tier diagram says is irreducible. You build it by using and auditing, every day.

props.heading: The daily loop.
props.body:
One real work task per day.
Use Claude on it.
Read every output critically.
Name one thing it got wrong or weak.

Not volume. Not novelty. Judgment-building.
Distrust-calibration is the irreducible skill.

beats.narration_text: What not to delegate. Facts you sign your name to — you verify before the signature. Judgment calls where you bear the consequence — the model does not bear consequences. Your professional reputation. Decisions your organization will act on without reviewing. The rule is simple: your name on it means your judgment owns it. Not your name on a form — your name as in you vouched for it. That is always Tier 4.

props.heading: Never delegate.
props.body:
— facts you sign your name to
— judgment calls where you bear the consequence
— your professional reputation
— decisions your org will act on without reviewing

Your name on it = your judgment owns it. Always Tier 4.

beats.narration_text: Commit before the reveal. Which of these four tasks is yours alone — meaning Claude can assist but the final call is entirely a human judgment that should not be delegated? A: draft an email. B: find the answer to a factual question. C: decide whether to fire a vendor. D: summarize a meeting transcript. Pick before the next beat.

props.command: which task is yours alone? A) draft an email B) find a fact C) fire a vendor D) summarize a transcript
props.runningText: commit to A, B, C, or D…

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. C. Firing a vendor is a consequential judgment call. You bear the cost, the relationship fallout, and the professional accountability. A, B, and D all have good AI-assist modes — draft, retrieve, summarize. None of them require you to waive your judgment. C is different: you might use Claude to research the situation, but the decision is entirely yours to own. That is what the tier diagram means by irreducible.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, On the Job — one card
artifactLines: The tier picture: Tier 1 is the machine's (recall, syntax). Tier 4 is yours (problem formulation, audit, distrust-calibration). Conduct, don't compete.
What's scarce: knowing what to ask, reading output critically, catching confabulations, deciding what reaches the human.
The daily loop: one real task, every output audited, one weak point named. That's judgment-building.
Never delegate: facts you sign your name to, judgment calls where you bear the consequence, your professional reputation.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude with one real task from today. After it gives you the output — read it critically and name one thing it got wrong or weak. That is your first audit rep.

props.command: Help me with [one real task from today's work]. After you give me the output, I'm going to tell you what I think you got wrong or weak — and you tell me if I'm right.
